questions.stem: A patient improving on day 3 of IV ceftriaxone for pyelonephritis is now afebrile, eating, and has a urine culture growing pan-sensitive E. coli. What is the best stewardship move?
options: Continue broad IV ceftriaxone for a full 14 days
De-escalate to a targeted oral agent (e.g. an oral cephalosporin or TMP-SMX per susceptibilities) and set a stop date
questions.explain: Once the organism, susceptibilities, and clinical improvement are known, de-escalate: narrow the spectrum, switch IV→PO, and set a defined stop date.

questions.stem: A septic patient needs antibiotics urgently, but the team wants blood cultures first. The blood-draw will take ~10 minutes; the patient is hypotensive. What is correct?
options: Delay antibiotics until cultures and susceptibilities fully return
Obtain cultures if it does not meaningfully delay therapy, but never delay lifesaving antibiotics in sepsis
Give antibiotics and never bother with cultures
questions.explain: Get cultures before antibiotics when feasible (improves yield), but in sepsis/meningitis do not delay empiric therapy — delays increase mortality.

questions.stem: A blood culture from a patient with an indwelling central line grows Gram-positive cocci in clusters identified as Staphylococcus aureus. How should this be regarded?
options: Almost certainly a skin contaminant — repeat and ignore
A true pathogen — S. aureus in blood is never dismissed as a contaminant
Only significant if the patient is febrile
questions.explain: S. aureus in blood is never dismissed as a contaminant — it implies true bacteremia (endocarditis/metastatic foci risk). Coagulase-negative staph is the common contaminant/biofilm organism.

questions.stem: A patient with a prosthetic hip has indolent infection; cultures grow coagulase-negative staphylococci forming a biofilm on the implant. Beyond antibiotics, what is often required for cure?
options: Nothing further — antibiotics alone clear biofilm
Source control: removal/replacement of the infected prosthetic material
Indefinite suppression without surgery in all cases
questions.explain: Biofilm on prosthetic material (CoNS, S. aureus, Candida) shields organisms from antibiotics; device removal/replacement (source control) is frequently needed.

questions.stem: A patient with suspected active pulmonary tuberculosis is admitted. Which precautions are required?
options: Airborne precautions: negative-pressure room and a fit-tested N95 respirator
Droplet precautions with a surgical mask only
questions.explain: TB is airborne — negative-pressure room, N95, door closed, plus public-health notification and contact tracing. The airborne trio to memorize: TB, measles, varicella.

questions.stem: A college student is admitted with meningococcal meningitis. Beyond treating the patient, what is the key public-health action for close contacts?
options: Offer chemoprophylaxis (post-exposure prophylaxis) to close contacts
Airborne isolation of all contacts for 21 days
Start all contacts on a 4-week antiretroviral course
questions.explain: Neisseria meningitidis spreads by droplets; close contacts should be offered PEP/chemoprophylaxis. The patient needs droplet precautions until 24 h of effective therapy.

questions.stem: On a ward, a patient with antibiotic-associated diarrhea is confirmed to have C. difficile. Which combination of measures is most appropriate?
options: Droplet precautions and alcohol gel only
Contact precautions, hand hygiene with soap and water, and sporicidal environmental cleaning
No precautions; C. difficile is not transmissible
questions.explain: C. difficile is spread by the fecal-oral/contact route and its spores resist alcohol — use contact precautions, soap-and-water hand hygiene, and sporicidal cleaning, plus antimicrobial stewardship.

questions.stem: A patient with bronchiectasis and a Pseudomonas-colonized airway develops pneumonia needing empiric anti-pseudomonal beta-lactam therapy. Which agent is appropriate?
questions.explain: Piperacillin-tazobactam (or ceftazidime/cefepime/meropenem) covers Pseudomonas. Cloxacillin/cephalexin/ampicillin do not.

questions.stem: A patient with MRSA bacteremia needs an appropriate empiric agent. Which is correct?
questions.explain: Vancomycin covers MRSA and resistant Gram-positives (no Gram-negative activity). The other listed agents don't reliably cover MRSA.

questions.stem: A patient on metronidazole for an anaerobic intra-abdominal infection asks whether he can have a beer. What should you advise and why?
options: Avoid alcohol — metronidazole can cause a disulfiram-like reaction
Only red wine is a problem
questions.explain: Metronidazole can cause a disulfiram-like reaction with alcohol (flushing, nausea, vomiting). Counsel avoidance; also note metallic taste and neuropathy with prolonged use.

questions.stem: A previously healthy adult is treated with clindamycin for a dental abscess and 10 days later develops profuse watery diarrhea. Which adverse effect is most likely?
questions.explain: Clindamycin carries a high C. difficile risk. (Tendon rupture is fluoroquinolones; nephrotoxicity is vancomycin/aminoglycosides; photosensitivity is doxycycline/FQ.)

questions.stem: A 9-year-old and a pregnant woman both need an antibiotic. Which agent should specifically be avoided in both because of effects on teeth and bone?
questions.explain: Doxycycline (tetracyclines) are avoided in pregnancy and in children <8 (dental staining/skeletal effects). Amoxicillin, azithromycin, and cephalexin are generally acceptable.

questions.stem: An ICU patient with central lines and recent broad-spectrum antibiotics grows Candida in blood cultures and is hemodynamically unstable. What is the best empiric antifungal and key concurrent step?
options: An echinocandin (e.g. caspofungin) plus removal/replacement of infected lines
questions.explain: For candidemia in an ill patient, start an echinocandin empirically and pursue line removal/replacement (source control), then step down to fluconazole if susceptible/stable and evaluate for dissemination.

questions.stem: A neutropenic patient with prolonged fever has CT findings suggestive of invasive pulmonary aspergillosis. The team considers fluconazole. What is the problem?
options: Fluconazole does not cover Aspergillus — voriconazole/posaconazole (or amphotericin) are needed
Aspergillus is best treated with an echinocandin alone as first-line
questions.explain: Fluconazole covers many Candida and Cryptococcus but NOT Aspergillus. Mould-active azoles (voriconazole/posaconazole) or amphotericin are required; echinocandins have only secondary activity against Aspergillus.

questions.stem: A patient asks why valacyclovir treats her genital herpes but would do nothing for influenza. What is the basis of acyclovir/valacyclovir selectivity for HSV/VZV?
options: They are nucleoside analogues activated by viral thymidine kinase and then inhibit viral DNA polymerase
questions.explain: Acyclovir/valacyclovir/famciclovir require viral thymidine kinase for activation and then inhibit the viral DNA polymerase — conferring HSV/VZV selectivity. Influenza needs a neuraminidase inhibitor (oseltamivir).

questions.stem: Before prescribing primaquine to clear P. vivax liver hypnozoites, which test must be checked, and why?
options: G6PD activity, because primaquine causes hemolysis in G6PD deficiency
TSH, because primaquine affects the thyroid
questions.explain: Check G6PD before primaquine — it triggers hemolysis in G6PD-deficient patients. Primaquine clears the dormant hypnozoites of P. vivax/ovale (not severe falciparum, which needs IV artesunate).

questions.stem: A newly diagnosed person with HIV is started on antiretroviral therapy. Which describes a typical initial regimen?
options: Two NRTIs plus an integrase strand-transfer inhibitor
questions.explain: A typical initial ART regimen is two NRTIs + an integrase inhibitor — combination therapy hits multiple life-cycle steps to suppress viral load, preserve immunity, and prevent transmission.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old with COPD is admitted with hypoxemic CAP (RR 30, confusion, SpO2 88%). Which empiric regimen best fits the course approach?
questions.explain: Admitted, hypoxemic, older CAP with CRB-65 features warrants inpatient therapy: ceftriaxone + azithromycin (atypical/Legionella cover). Don't delay antibiotics; add vancomycin only if MRSA concern or pip-tazo if Pseudomonas risk.

questions.stem: A CAP patient stabilizes and the blood culture returns penicillin-sensitive Streptococcus pneumoniae. What is the best next step?
options: Narrow to amoxicillin/penicillin and plan IV-to-PO switch
questions.explain: Targeted de-escalation: penicillin-sensitive pneumococcus → narrow to amoxicillin/penicillin, switch IV→PO once improving, finish an appropriate (usually 5–7 day) course.

questions.stem: A woman from a high-incidence country has 2 months of cough, fevers, night sweats, and weight loss, unimproved after amoxicillin-clavulanate and a respiratory fluoroquinolone. What is the priority diagnostic and infection-control step?
options: Repeat the same antibiotics at home
Airborne isolation and sputum ×3 for AFB smear/culture + NAAT, with public-health notification
IGRA to diagnose active disease and discharge
questions.explain: Non-resolving 'pneumonia' with chronic constitutional symptoms from a high-incidence region is TB until proven otherwise: airborne isolation + sputum ×3 AFB/culture + NAAT, notify public health, contact-trace. IGRA/TST diagnose latent infection, not active disease.

questions.stem: A 75-year-old with pyelonephritis is drowsy with RR 30 and SBP 100. Which plan best fits sepsis management?
options: Oral antibiotics and discharge because SBP >90
IV fluids, blood and urine cultures, organ-dysfunction labs, and early empiric IV antibiotics
questions.explain: She meets qSOFA (RR ≥22, altered mentation). Sepsis care targets infection and the dysregulated host response: cultures, early empiric IV antibiotics, IV fluids/perfusion, lactate/organ monitoring, and source control.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old presents with fever, neck stiffness, and confusion; bacterial meningitis is suspected. Which empiric regimen best matches the course approach for adults, and why is the third agent included?
options: Ceftriaxone + vancomycin + ampicillin — ampicillin adds Listeria cover in older/at-risk adults
questions.explain: Adult bacterial meningitis empiric therapy: ceftriaxone (common pathogens) + vancomycin (resistant pneumococcus) + ampicillin for Listeria in older/immunocompromised/pregnant adults. Don't delay for the LP.

questions.stem: An older inpatient develops watery diarrhea after antibiotics; C. difficile toxin is positive. Which treatment fits current course guidance?
questions.explain: Current CDI therapy favours oral vancomycin or fidaxomicin (metronidazole is now less preferred). Recurrent disease may need fecal microbiota transplant; severe complications may need colectomy.

questions.stem: A diabetic man has a red, hot, tender leg with a clear portal of entry at a cracked, scaly web space between the toes. Besides treating the cellulitis, what should be addressed?
options: Treat the tinea pedis portal of entry
questions.explain: Cellulitis often has a portal of entry — here tinea pedis. Treating it reduces recurrence. Manage cellulitis with cephalexin/cefazolin and reassess.

questions.stem: A man has severe leg pain out of proportion to exam, rapidly advancing erythema, tense bullae, fever, and tachycardia. What is the immediate priority?
options: Urgent surgical consultation for debridement plus broad empiric antibiotics
Wait for a superficial wound swab
questions.explain: Necrotizing fasciitis — pain out of proportion, rapid spread, bullae, systemic toxicity — is a surgical emergency. Immediate surgical debridement + broad antibiotics (penicillin + clindamycin for GAS); imaging/cultures must not delay surgery.

questions.stem: Blood cultures from an ICU patient grow Candida albicans. How should this result be interpreted?
options: Treat as candidemia/invasive candidiasis — Candida in blood is never a contaminant
Repeat once and ignore if the second is negative
questions.explain: Candida in blood is never dismissed as a contaminant. Treat as candidemia: systemic antifungal (echinocandin first in ill patients), remove/replace lines, and assess for disseminated foci.

questions.stem: Serology: HBsAg negative, anti-HBc positive, anti-HBs positive, IgM anti-HBc negative; anti-HCV positive. Which interpretation and next test are correct?
options: Acute hepatitis B; no HCV testing needed
Resolved natural hepatitis B, plus HCV exposure that requires HCV RNA to determine active infection
Hepatitis B vaccine immunity; HCV cured
Chronic hepatitis B and HCV immunity
questions.explain: HBsAg−, anti-HBc+, anti-HBs+, IgM− = resolved natural HBV infection (vaccine immunity would lack anti-HBc). Anti-HCV+ means exposure only — order HCV RNA to confirm active infection.

questions.stem: A medical student with a documented complete hepatitis B vaccine series has anti-HBs of 4 IU/L, with negative HBsAg and anti-HBc. What does this indicate?
options: Inadequate vaccine response — booster/repeat series with repeat serology per occupational-health policy
questions.explain: Anti-HBs <10 IU/L after vaccination indicates an inadequate/non-responder state; negative HBsAg/anti-HBc argues against current or past natural infection. Occupational health uses a booster/repeat series with repeat titres.

questions.stem: A patient with chronic hepatitis B and detectable HBV DNA is started on antiviral suppression. Which agents are first-line?
questions.explain: Chronic HBV suppression uses nucleos(t)ide analogues — tenofovir or entecavir — monitored with HBV DNA. Acyclovir is for HSV/VZV; oseltamivir for influenza.

questions.stem: A 39-year-old has 8 days of fever, sore throat, diffuse maculopapular rash, diarrhea, and tender cervical lymphadenopathy, 3 weeks after condomless sex with a new partner. Which tests best evaluate the leading diagnosis?
options: HIV 1/2 antibody/p24 antigen test plus HIV viral load
questions.explain: This is classic acute HIV seroconversion. Test with a 4th-gen Ab/p24 antigen assay plus HIV viral load — HIV RNA is detectable earliest, before antibodies fully develop.

questions.stem: A nurse sustains a percutaneous needlestick from a patient with HIV who has been on ART for years with a documented undetectable viral load and no concomitant STI. What does the course framework say about PEP?
options: PEP is mandatory for every HIV-source needlestick regardless of viral load
PEP is generally not recommended when the source is virologically suppressed with no STI (negligible transmissible risk); still perform baseline/follow-up testing
A single dose of one antiretroviral suffices
PEP can wait until 10 days after exposure
questions.explain: PEP decisions weigh source risk and exposure type. A virologically suppressed source with no STI poses negligible transmissible risk, so PEP is generally not recommended; when indicated, PEP is 3 antiretrovirals for 4 weeks, started within 72 h. Baseline and 6/12-week testing still apply.

questions.stem: A man presents for HIV PEP for the third time in 6 months after repeated high-risk exposures. Beyond this PEP course, what should be offered?
options: Nothing — repeated PEP is the expected long-term strategy
questions.explain: Recurrent high-risk exposures needing repeated PEP should prompt counselling and an offer of PrEP rather than relying on serial post-exposure courses.

questions.stem: A physician returns from rural Kenya and develops fever, headache, and malaise 5 days later, having taken malaria prophylaxis inconsistently. What is the first diagnostic priority?
options: Rule out malaria now with thick/thin smears (repeat q12h ×3) or NAAT
questions.explain: Malaria must be ruled out first in any febrile traveller from the tropics (esp. sub-Saharan Africa) — thick/thin smears now, repeated q12h ×3, or NAAT. It is the key life-threatening, treatable diagnosis.

questions.stem: A traveller from Cameroon has P. falciparum on smear and is confused, hypoglycemic, and in acute kidney injury. Which management is correct?
options: Urgent parenteral therapy with IV artesunate plus critical-care management
questions.explain: Severe falciparum malaria (cerebral involvement, hypoglycemia, AKI) needs IV artesunate and ICU-level care. Primaquine targets P. vivax/ovale hypnozoites, not severe falciparum.

questions.stem: A patient with gastroenteritis has rigors and feels intensely cold despite a measured temperature of 38.7 °C. Which mechanism explains the rigors?
options: Pyrogens lower the set point and shivering cools the body
Pyrogen-induced cytokines raise PGE2 and the hypothalamic set point; shivering generates heat toward the new, higher set point
questions.explain: Pyrogens → leukocyte cytokines (IL-1/TNF/IL-6) → PGE2 → raised hypothalamic set point. The body now perceives itself as cold and shivers (rigors) to generate heat.

questions.stem: A previously healthy man with bilateral pneumonia worsens after 4 days of ceftriaxone + azithromycin; cultures and atypical/viral testing are negative and labs show profound lymphopenia. What is the best next step?
options: Send HIV testing and consider Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia
Stop investigating because sputum grew normal flora
questions.explain: Non-resolving pneumonia means re-examine bug/drug/diagnosis/host/source. Profound lymphopenia with worsening diffuse pneumonia should prompt HIV testing and consideration of PJP (an AIDS-defining opportunistic infection).

cards.prompt: Order the ID case approach:
cards.explain: Syndrome → bugs/host → tests → empiric → reassess/narrow.

cards.prompt: Which are core stewardship actions? (select all)
options: Switch IV to PO when improving
Continue the broadest agent for a fixed 14 days regardless
cards.explain: De-escalate, IV→PO, stop date; don't reflexively run broad agents.

cards.prompt: When is it acceptable to give antibiotics before cultures?
cards.answer: In sepsis/meningitis (and other life-threatening infection) — never delay lifesaving antibiotics for cultures, though you obtain cultures first whenever it doesn't meaningfully delay therapy.

cards.prompt: Name four situations that require source control.
cards.answer: Drain an abscess, remove an infected line/device, debride necrotizing infection, control a perforated viscus (also: relieve obstruction, drain empyema).

cards.prompt: Match the Gram morphology to an organism:
cards.explain: Shape + Gram reaction narrows the differential.

cards.prompt: Why is S. aureus in a blood culture never dismissed as a contaminant, while CoNS often is?
cards.answer: S. aureus bacteremia is virulent (endocarditis, metastatic seeding) and must be treated; coagulase-negative staph is common skin flora and a frequent contaminant/biofilm organism.

cards.text: Yeasts such as {{Candida}} and Cryptococcus are unicellular budding fungi; {{Aspergillus}} is a mould. The simplest first split for viruses is {{DNA vs RNA}}.
cards.explain: Fungi: yeast vs mould; viruses: nucleic acid first.

cards.prompt: Why does biofilm on a prosthetic device often require device removal?
cards.answer: Biofilm (CoNS, S. aureus, Candida) shields organisms from antibiotics and host defenses, so antibiotics alone frequently fail — source control by removing/replacing the device is needed.

cards.prompt: Antibiotic-driven dysbiosis predisposes to:
cards.explain: Loss of colonization resistance from antibiotics enables C. difficile and Candida overgrowth.

cards.prompt: Match the pathogen to its precaution:
cards.explain: Match mode to precaution; airborne = TB, measles, varicella.

cards.text: The three classic airborne infections requiring N95 and negative pressure are {{TB}}, {{measles}}, and {{varicella}}.
cards.explain: Everything else respiratory is usually droplet.

cards.prompt: List four public-health tools used to control communicable disease.
cards.answer: Vaccination, post-exposure prophylaxis, reportable-disease notification, contact tracing, quarantine, outbreak investigation, surveillance (any four).

cards.prompt: What is offered to close contacts of a meningococcal meningitis case, and why?
cards.answer: Chemoprophylaxis/PEP — N. meningitidis is droplet-transmitted and close contacts are at risk; the index case needs droplet precautions until 24 h of effective therapy.

cards.prompt: Match the beta-lactam to its niche:
cards.explain: Know who covers Pseudomonas, MSSA, and Listeria/Enterococcus.

cards.prompt: Vancomycin's spectrum is best summarized as:
options: MRSA and resistant Gram-positives, no Gram-negative
cards.explain: Glycopeptide for MRSA/resistant Gram-positives; no Gram-negative activity.

cards.prompt: Match the antibiotic to its signature toxicity:
cards.explain: Toxicity fingerprints are high-yield exam discriminators.

cards.prompt: Why is clindamycin added to penicillin in invasive group A strep / necrotizing fasciitis?
cards.answer: Clindamycin has an anti-toxin (protein-synthesis-inhibiting) effect that suppresses GAS exotoxin production, in addition to its antibacterial action.

cards.text: Doxycycline is avoided in {{pregnancy}} and in children under {{8}} due to dental staining and skeletal effects.
cards.explain: Tetracycline class caution.

cards.prompt: Which antibiotic classes cover atypical pneumonia organisms (Mycoplasma, Chlamydophila, Legionella)?
cards.answer: Macrolides (azithromycin), respiratory fluoroquinolones (levofloxacin/moxifloxacin), and tetracyclines (doxycycline).

cards.prompt: Match the antifungal to its key point:
cards.explain: Echinocandin first for candidemia; mould azoles for Aspergillus.

cards.prompt: What is the empiric antifungal of choice for candidemia in an unstable patient, and the key non-drug step?
cards.answer: An echinocandin (caspofungin/micafungin/anidulafungin), plus removal/replacement of infected lines/devices; step down to fluconazole if susceptible and stable.

cards.prompt: Flucytosine is typically combined with amphotericin to treat:
cards.explain: Flucytosine + amphotericin for cryptococcal disease; not as monotherapy (resistance).

cards.prompt: Why do acyclovir/valacyclovir selectively treat HSV/VZV?
cards.answer: They are nucleoside analogues that require activation by viral thymidine kinase, then inhibit the viral DNA polymerase — conferring selectivity (and TK mutations cause resistance).

cards.text: Influenza is treated with neuraminidase inhibitors such as {{oseltamivir}}; chronic HBV is suppressed with nucleos(t)ides such as {{tenofovir}} or entecavir.
cards.explain: Match antiviral to virus.

cards.text: A typical initial HIV regimen is two {{NRTIs}} plus an {{integrase}} inhibitor; goals include viral suppression and prevention of {{transmission}}.
cards.explain: Combination ART hits multiple steps.

cards.prompt: What must be checked before primaquine, and what does primaquine target?
cards.answer: Check G6PD (hemolysis risk). Primaquine clears the dormant liver hypnozoites of P. vivax and P. ovale.

cards.prompt: Treatment of severe falciparum malaria is:
cards.explain: Severe malaria → IV artesunate; supportive ICU care.

cards.text: CRB-65 scores {{Confusion}}, RR ≥{{30}}, low BP, and age ≥{{65}}. The most common CAP organism is {{S. pneumoniae}}.
cards.explain: CRB-65 stratifies CAP severity/disposition.

cards.prompt: How do CAP and HAP differ in likely organisms?
cards.answer: CAP: S. pneumoniae (plus H. influenzae, Moraxella, atypicals, post-flu S. aureus). HAP: more Gram-negative bacilli, Pseudomonas, and S. aureus — needing broader/anti-pseudomonal empiric cover.

cards.prompt: Severe/admitted CAP empiric therapy typically pairs a beta-lactam with:
cards.explain: Ceftriaxone + azithromycin covers typical + atypical organisms.

cards.prompt: Which tests diagnose latent vs active pulmonary TB?
cards.answer: Latent: TST or IGRA. Active pulmonary: CXR plus sputum ×3 for AFB smear/culture with susceptibilities, and NAAT. TST/IGRA do not diagnose active disease in adults.

cards.prompt: Which apply to suspected active pulmonary TB? (select all)
cards.explain: Airborne control, notification/tracing, sputum ×3; not routine CAP antibiotics.

cards.text: qSOFA flags risk with ≥2 of: RR ≥{{22}}, altered {{mentation}}, and SBP ≤{{100}}. Septic shock adds vasopressor need and elevated {{lactate}} despite fluids.
cards.explain: qSOFA is a bedside screen, not a diagnosis.

cards.prompt: Outline the core early sepsis bundle.
cards.answer: Obtain cultures quickly, give early appropriate broad-spectrum antibiotics, give IV fluids to restore perfusion and clear lactate, add vasopressors for shock, monitor lactate/organ function, and achieve source control. Don't delay antibiotics.

cards.prompt: What is the empiric regimen for adult bacterial meningitis, and what does each component cover?
cards.answer: Ceftriaxone (common pathogens, e.g. pneumococcus/meningococcus) + vancomycin (resistant pneumococcus) + ampicillin (Listeria in older/immunocompromised/pregnant adults). Don't delay for the LP.

cards.prompt: Why does C. difficile recur, and how is it treated first-line now?
cards.answer: Its spores resist alcohol and harsh environments (and persist in colonic crypts), driving recurrence. First-line: oral vancomycin or fidaxomicin (metronidazole less preferred); recurrent disease may need fecal microbiota transplant.

cards.prompt: Most non-C. difficile infectious diarrhea is best managed with:
options: Supportive hydration; antibiotics only when indicated
cards.explain: Most acute infectious diarrhea is self-limited; avoid unnecessary antibiotics.

cards.prompt: Match the SSTI to a management point:
cards.explain: I&D for abscess, surgery for nec fasc, find the portal in cellulitis.

cards.prompt: What clinical clues flag necrotizing fasciitis over simple cellulitis?
cards.answer: Pain out of proportion to exam, rapid progression, bullae, systemic toxicity, and crepitus/skin necrosis — a surgical emergency needing debridement, not just antibiotics.

cards.prompt: List several risk factors for invasive candidiasis.
cards.answer: Central lines, TPN, broad-spectrum antibiotics, prolonged ICU stay, abdominal surgery, neutropenia/hematologic malignancy, diabetes, organ transplant.

cards.text: Candida in blood is {{never}} a contaminant. Empiric therapy in an ill patient is an {{echinocandin}}, plus removal of infected {{lines}}.
cards.explain: Candidemia: treat, source-control, assess dissemination.

cards.prompt: Match the HBV marker to its meaning:
cards.explain: anti-HBc distinguishes natural infection from vaccine immunity.

cards.prompt: A patient is HBsAg−, anti-HBc+, anti-HBs+, IgM anti-HBc−. Interpretation?
cards.answer: Resolved natural HBV infection (vaccine immunity would have anti-HBs but NOT anti-HBc).

cards.prompt: A positive anti-HCV tells you what, and what test confirms active infection?
cards.answer: It indicates HCV exposure (could be acute, chronic, or resolved). HCV RNA is needed to confirm active viremia/infection.

cards.text: Protective hepatitis B immunity is anti-HBs ≥{{10}} IU/L. Chronic HBV risk is ~{{90}}% after infant infection but only ~5% after adult infection.
cards.explain: Age at infection drives chronicity risk.

cards.prompt: Which hepatitis virus has the highest rate of progression to chronic infection (but is usually curable)?
cards.explain: HCV: ~75–85% chronic, but modern DAAs cure most. HAV never chronic.

cards.prompt: In acute HIV, which marker becomes detectable earliest, and what is the screening test?
cards.answer: HIV RNA (viral load) is detectable earliest, then p24 antigen, then antibodies. Screen with a 4th-generation HIV antibody/p24-antigen assay.

cards.prompt: When should ART be started in a newly diagnosed HIV patient?
cards.answer: For all people with HIV, regardless of CD4 count — don't wait for CD4 <200. Early ART preserves immunity and prevents transmission.

cards.text: HIV PEP should start within {{72}} hours (best <24 h) and consists of {{3}} antiretrovirals for {{4}} weeks; follow-up testing at 6 and 12 weeks.
cards.explain: Time-critical, 3-drug, 4-week course.

cards.prompt: A source patient on ART has a documented undetectable viral load and no STI. How does this affect PEP?
cards.answer: Transmissible risk is negligible, so PEP is generally not recommended even for higher-risk exposures; baseline and follow-up testing still apply.

cards.prompt: What is the immediate response to a needlestick injury?
cards.answer: Stop, wash/flush the area, and report immediately to a supervisor/occupational health/ED — do not wait until the end of shift; then assess source and exposure for PEP decisions.

cards.prompt: Which are true about hepatitis post-exposure? (select all)
options: HBV: check anti-HBs; vaccine ± HBIG if nonimmune and source positive/unknown
HCV: no vaccine or prophylaxis; baseline + follow-up testing
cards.explain: No HCV vaccine/Ig/prophylaxis exists; HBV uses vaccine ± HBIG; anti-HBs ≥10 is protective.

cards.text: Pyrogens trigger leukocyte cytokines (IL-1/TNF/IL-6) → {{PGE2}} → a {{raised}} hypothalamic set point; the body then {{shivers}} (rigors) to reach it.
cards.explain: Set-point physiology explains rigors.

cards.prompt: Besides infection, name causes on the fever differential.
cards.answer: Malignancy, autoimmune/inflammatory disease, drug fever, thrombosis (DVT/PE), endocrine/metabolic, and environmental heat illness. Fever may be blunted in older adults.

cards.prompt: What is the first diagnosis to exclude in any febrile traveller from the tropics, and how?
cards.answer: Malaria — with thick/thin blood smears now, repeated q12h up to 3 sets, or malaria NAAT. It is the key life-threatening, treatable cause.

cards.prompt: Match the incubation to likely diagnoses:
cards.explain: Incubation narrows the traveller differential.

cards.prompt: What is the simplest clinically useful first split for classifying human viruses?
cards.answer: DNA versus RNA (then further by envelope, capsid symmetry, and replication strategy). Enveloped viruses are generally less environmentally hardy.

cards.prompt: Match the parasite host term to its definition:
cards.explain: Host terminology for parasite life cycles.

cards.prompt: List several drivers of infectious-disease emergence.
cards.answer: Microbial adaptation/change, human susceptibility, economic development/land use, climate/weather, travel/commerce, breakdown of public-health measures, and intent to harm (bioterrorism).

cards.text: Pandemic influenza arises from a novel virus via antigenic {{shift}}/reassortment, giving higher attack rates than seasonal flu.
cards.explain: Shift/reassortment = pandemic potential; drift = seasonal.

cards.prompt: Match the non-resolving-infection problem to an example:
cards.explain: Re-think bug, drug, source, diagnosis, and host.

cards.prompt: Worsening bilateral pneumonia with profound lymphopenia despite standard CAP therapy — what host-factor work-up is key?
cards.answer: Test for HIV and consider Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia (an opportunistic, AIDS-defining infection).

cards.prompt: Which beta-lactams add Listeria/Enterococcus coverage that cephalosporins lack?
cards.answer: Aminopenicillins — ampicillin/amoxicillin. (Cephalosporins generally do not cover Listeria or enterococci.)

cards.prompt: Additional (transmission-based) precautions are added when:
options: The syndrome/pathogen suggests extra transmission risk (contact, droplet, or airborne)
Never, routine practices suffice for all
cards.explain: Routine practices for all; additional precautions layered by transmission risk.

cards.prompt: How are bacteria classically classified in the lab?
cards.answer: By Gram reaction (positive/negative), morphology (cocci/bacilli; clusters/chains), and oxygen tolerance (aerobic/anaerobic).

cards.prompt: Which lab pattern reflects impaired hepatic synthetic function in hepatitis?
cards.answer: Low albumin and a prolonged PT/INR (in addition to elevated bilirubin and transaminases, which reflect injury rather than synthesis).

cards.prompt: Match the emerging disease to a key feature:
cards.explain: Each emerging/re-emerging disease has a signature reservoir/transmission.

cards.prompt: Name the malaria chemoprophylaxis options and a caution for each.
cards.answer: Atovaquone-proguanil (GI/cost), doxycycline (photosensitivity/esophagitis, not in pregnancy/young children), and mefloquine (neuropsychiatric effects). Choice depends on destination/resistance/patient.

cards.text: Per-exposure HIV transmission risk (course figures): percutaneous ~{{0.3}}%, mucous membrane ~{{0.1}}%, intact skin <0.1%; risk rises with source {{viral load}}.
cards.explain: Exposure type and source viral load drive risk.

cards.prompt: Oseltamivir treats influenza by inhibiting:
cards.explain: Oseltamivir is a neuraminidase inhibitor; baloxavir inhibits the cap-dependent endonuclease.

cards.prompt: For what infections is TMP-SMX used, and what are key cautions?
cards.answer: Some MRSA SSTIs, Pneumocystis (PJP) treatment/prophylaxis, and many UTIs. Cautions: sulfa allergy/SJS, hyperkalemia, cytopenias, and use-with-care in pregnancy.

cards.prompt: Name two cautions specific to carbapenems (e.g. meropenem).
cards.answer: Seizure/CNS risk and a drug interaction that lowers valproic acid levels; reserve for strong indications (e.g. severe ESBL infection). Ertapenem lacks Pseudomonas coverage.

cards.prompt: What does an isolated positive anti-HBc (HBsAg−, anti-HBs−) mean?
cards.answer: Several possibilities: resolved infection with waning anti-HBs, a false positive, occult HBV infection, or the window period — interpret clinically and with further testing.

cards.text: {{Enveloped}} viruses are generally less environmentally hardy than non-enveloped viruses. Active viral disease is often confirmed by {{NAAT}}/PCR or antigen detection.
cards.explain: Envelope affects environmental survival; NAAT detects active disease.

cards.prompt: A febrile traveller who returned 10 days ago — which becomes LESS likely?
cards.explain: Dengue has a short incubation (often 3–5 days); if symptom onset is >7 days after return it becomes less likely.

cards.prompt: Aminoglycosides (e.g. gentamicin) require monitoring chiefly because of:
cards.explain: Nephro- and ototoxicity (and neuromuscular blockade) necessitate level monitoring.

cards.prompt: Give one benefit and one harm of the normal microbiota.
cards.answer: Benefit: immune stimulation and colonization resistance against pathogens. Harm: a source of contamination or opportunistic infection when displaced into sterile sites (e.g. translocation, line infection).

cards.prompt: Match the antifungal class to its mechanism:
cards.explain: Target the membrane, its synthesis, or the cell wall.

cards.prompt: What is the empiric antibiotic of choice for animal/human bite wounds, and why?
cards.answer: Amoxicillin-clavulanate — bites are polymicrobial including anaerobes and oral flora (e.g. Pasteurella), which it covers; moxifloxacin is an alternative in some settings.

cards.prompt: Define sepsis vs septic shock, with their approximate mortality.
cards.answer: Sepsis = life-threatening organ dysfunction from a dysregulated host response to infection (SOFA ≥2; mortality >10%). Septic shock = sepsis with vasopressor requirement and elevated lactate despite fluid resuscitation (mortality >40%).

cards.text: Acute HIV seroconversion presents with fever, {{lymphadenopathy}}, pharyngitis, {{rash}}, and diarrhea after exposure; untreated HIV progresses over roughly {{10}} years.
cards.explain: A mononucleosis-like syndrome should prompt HIV testing in the right context.

cards.prompt: Why obtain cultures before starting antibiotics when feasible?
cards.answer: Antibiotics rapidly reduce culture yield; pre-treatment blood/urine/sputum/CSF/abscess cultures improve the chance of identifying the organism and susceptibilities to enable later de-escalation — but never delay lifesaving therapy to get them.

guides.title: Weeks 12–13 · 1 ID Framework & Stewardship
guides.html:
Approach

ID Framework & Stewardship

Weeks 12–13 · Micro & ID
CPC 1 · Year 1
Study Suite

The seven-step approach applied to every infection case, and the stewardship questions — empiric vs targeted, broad vs narrow, cultures-first, source control, and de-escalation.

The 7-step approach

Use the same scaffold for every case: syndrome → likely bugs → host risk factors → diagnostics → empiric therapy → reassess/de-escalate → prevention/transmission control.

  1. Is there an infection? Localizing + systemic signs, inflammatory markers, imaging, micro. Fever is not always infection.
  2. What is the syndrome? (pneumonia, gastroenteritis, SSTI, sepsis, traveller's fever…)
  3. Bug story: likely pathogens, reservoir, portal of entry, source-control need.
  4. Patient story: age, pregnancy, immunosuppression, diabetes, devices, travel, exposures.
  5. Communicable? Routine practices + additional precautions; public-health notification/contact tracing.
  6. Confirm & manage: order tests that change management; treat promptly (esp. sepsis/meningitis) but avoid unnecessary antimicrobials.
  7. Reassess at 48–72 h / on cultures: narrow, IV→PO, set a stop date.

Stewardship questions

AskMeaning
Bacterial — confirmed or suspected?No antibacterials for uncomplicated viral URTI
Empiric or targeted?Empiric if ill/pending; targeted once organism + susceptibilities known
Broad or narrow?Start broad only when justified; narrow ASAP
Cultures first?Blood/urine/sputum/CSF/abscess before antibiotics when feasible — but never delay lifesaving antibiotics
Source control?Drain abscess, remove infected line/device, debride necrotizing infection, control perforation
Route/dose/duration?IV for severe/can't absorb; switch to PO once improving; shortest effective duration
De-escalation (narrow spectrum, IV→PO, stop date) is the heart of antimicrobial stewardship.

guides.title: Weeks 12–13 · 2 Microbiology Basics & Gram Stain
guides.html:
Bugs 101

Microbiology Basics & Gram Stain

Weeks 12–13 · Micro & ID
CPC 1 · Year 1
Study Suite

Organism classes, the Gram-stain/morphology map from staph clusters to Gram-negative bacilli (with the contaminant caveats), and virulence factors, biofilm, and dysbiosis.

Organism classes

ClassHigh-yield
BacteriaProkaryote, peptidoglycan wall; classify by Gram, morphology, O₂ tolerance
VirusesAcellular, obligate intracellular; classify by nucleic acid (DNA/RNA), envelope, capsid, replication
FungiYeasts (Candida, Cryptococcus) vs moulds/dimorphic (Aspergillus, Mucor, Histoplasma, Blastomyces)
ParasitesProtozoa, helminths, arthropods (Plasmodium → malaria)

Gram stain & morphology

Gram / shapeExamplesClinical clue
GPC in clustersS. aureus; CoNSS. aureus: SSTI, bacteremia, post-flu pneumonia. Never dismiss S. aureus in blood as contaminant; CoNS often contaminant/biofilm
GPC in chainsS. pyogenes, S. agalactiae, Enterococcus; S. pneumoniae (diplococci)GAS: cellulitis/nec fasc/TSS; pneumococcus: CAP/meningitis
GP bacilliListeria, Corynebacterium, Bacillus, ClostridiumC. difficile spores → antibiotic-associated colitis
GN diplococciNeisseriaMeningitis/meningococcemia; gonorrhea
GN coccobacilliHaemophilus, MoraxellaCAP/COPD exacerbation
GN bacilliE. coli, Klebsiella, Pseudomonas, BacteroidesUTI, sepsis, HAP, intra-abdominal; Bacteroides anaerobic; Pseudomonas with structural lung/healthcare

Virulence & microbiota

  • Virulence: invasion (adhesins, capsule, biofilm, intracellular survival) + host damage (inflammation, toxins).
  • Biofilm on catheters/prosthetics (CoNS, S. aureus, Candida) → may need device removal.
  • Dysbiosis (often from antimicrobials) → C. difficile, candidiasis.

guides.title: Weeks 12–13 · 3 Transmission, Infection Control & Public Health
guides.html:
Stop the Spread

Transmission, Infection Control & Public Health

Weeks 12–13 · Micro & ID
CPC 1 · Year 1
Study Suite

The five transmission modes and their precautions (with the airborne trio — TB, measles, varicella), routine vs additional precautions, and the public-health toolkit.

Transmission modes & precautions

ModeExamplesPrecautions
Contact / fecal-oralNorovirus, C. difficile, resistant organismsGown/gloves, hand hygiene; sporicidal cleaning for C. diff
Droplet (~2 m)Influenza, respiratory viruses, N. meningitidisSurgical mask + eye protection; close-contact PEP for meningococcus
AirborneTB, measles, varicellaNegative-pressure room + N95, door closed; notify public health, contact-trace
Vector-borneMalaria, Zika, West Nile, LymeVector avoidance, travel counselling, surveillance
Blood-borneNeedlestick, transfusion, IDU, sexual, verticalImmediate reporting, baseline testing, PEP when indicated
Memorize the airborne three
TB, measles, varicella → N95 + negative pressure. Everything else respiratory is usually droplet.

Infection control & public health

  • Routine practices: hand hygiene, risk assessment, PPE, safe sharps, environmental cleaning.
  • Additional precautions: contact, droplet/contact, or airborne by syndrome/pathogen.
  • Public-health tools: vaccination, PEP, reportable-disease notification, contact tracing, quarantine, outbreak investigation, surveillance.
  • Stewardship of resources: avoid low-value testing/treatment (harm, false positives, cost).

guides.title: Weeks 12–13 · 4 Antibacterial Pharmacology
guides.html:
Drugs I

Antibacterial Pharmacology

Weeks 12–13 · Micro & ID
CPC 1 · Year 1
Study Suite

Beta-lactams from amoxicillin to carbapenems (who covers Pseudomonas, Enterococcus/Listeria, MSSA), and the key non-beta-lactams with their signature toxicities (vancomycin, FQs, metronidazole disulfiram, clindamycin→C. diff).

Beta-lactams

DrugSpectrum pearlCaution
Amoxicillin/ampicillinStrep, Enterococcus, ListeriaAllergy; C. diff
Cloxacillin / cefazolinMSSANot MRSA
Piperacillin-tazobactamBroad incl. Pseudomonas + anaerobesBroad — reserve
CeftriaxoneCAP, meningitis, pyeloAvoid in neonates
Ceftazidime / cefepimePseudomonas—
Carbapenems (meropenem)Very broad; ESBLSeizure risk; ↓valproate; reserve

Other key antibacterials

DrugUseSignature toxicity
VancomycinMRSA, resistant Gram-positives (no Gram-neg)Nephrotoxicity; monitor levels
Azithromycin (macrolide)Atypicals (Mycoplasma, Chlamydophila, Legionella)QT prolongation
Fluoroquinolones (levo/moxi/cipro)Respiratory FQ (levo/moxi); cipro = Pseudomonas, weak pneumococcusTendon rupture, QT, dysglycemia, aortic aneurysm, C. diff
DoxycyclineAtypicals, rickettsia, malaria ppxAvoid pregnancy & children <8; photosensitivity
Aminoglycosides (gentamicin)Gram-negative synergyNephro/ototoxicity; monitor
MetronidazoleAnaerobes, Giardia, Entamoeba, TrichomonasDisulfiram-like reaction (no alcohol); neuropathy
ClindamycinGP/anaerobe; anti-toxin in invasive GAS/nec fascHigh C. difficile risk
TMP-SMXSome MRSA, PJP, UTISulfa allergy/SJS, hyperkalemia, cytopenias

guides.title: Weeks 12–13 · 5 Antifungal, Antiviral & Antiparasitic
guides.html:
Drugs II

Antifungal, Antiviral & Antiparasitic

Weeks 12–13 · Micro & ID
CPC 1 · Year 1
Study Suite

Antifungals (echinocandin-first candidemia, fluconazole's Aspergillus gap, amphotericin toxicity), antivirals/ART (acyclovir needs viral TK, HBV nucleos(t)ides, 2-NRTI+INSTI), and antiparasitics (IV artesunate, G6PD before primaquine).

Antifungals

DrugMechanism / useCaution
Amphotericin BBinds ergosterol → membrane pores; broad, severe invasive diseaseInfusion reactions, nephrotoxicity, ↓K/↓Mg; liposomal less toxic
Azoles (fluconazole, vori/posaconazole)Inhibit ergosterol synthesis. Fluconazole: Candida + Cryptococcus, NOT Aspergillus; vori/posa cover AspergillusHepatotoxicity, QT, big CYP interactions
Echinocandins (caspofungin)Inhibit β-1,3-glucan; cidal for Candida; empiric for candidemia in ill patientsIV only
TerbinafineDermatophytes (nails/hair)Hepatotoxicity
FlucytosineWith amphotericin for CryptococcusMarrow suppression

Antivirals & ART

DrugUse / mechanismCaution
Acyclovir/valacyclovirHSV/VZV polymerase inhibitor; needs viral thymidine kinase activationCrystalluria/nephrotoxicity — hydrate
Ganciclovir; foscarnet; cidofovirCMVGanciclovir: myelosuppression; foscarnet: nephro/electrolytes
Oseltamivir; baloxavirInfluenza (neuraminidase / cap-endonuclease)GI; resistance
Tenofovir, entecavirHBV nucleos(t)ide polymerase inhibitorsTenofovir renal/bone
HCV DAAs (sofosbuvir, …)NS5B/NS5A/NS3-4A; cure most HCVInteractions; screen HBV reactivation
HIV ARTInitial = 2 NRTIs + integrase inhibitor; suppresses load, prevents transmissionTenofovir renal/bone; abacavir hypersensitivity

Antiparasitics

Malaria
Severe malaria → IV artesunate + critical care. Hypnozoites of P. vivax/ovale → primaquine — check G6PD first (hemolysis). Prophylaxis: atovaquone-proguanil, doxycycline, or mefloquine.

Protozoa: metronidazole (Giardia/Entamoeba/Trichomonas), nitazoxanide (Crypto). Helminths: albendazole/mebendazole, ivermectin, praziquantel.

guides.title: Weeks 12–13 · 6 Pneumonia, TB, Sepsis & Meningitis
guides.html:
Severe Syndromes

Pneumonia, TB, Sepsis & Meningitis

Weeks 12–13 · Micro & ID
CPC 1 · Year 1
Study Suite

CAP vs HAP bugs and empiric therapy with CRB-65, the TB work-up and airborne control, and the sepsis bundle plus the adult bacterial-meningitis empiric triple (ceftriaxone + vancomycin + ampicillin).

Pneumonia (CAP vs HAP)

BugsEmpiric
CAPS. pneumoniae #1; H. influenzae, Moraxella; atypicals; post-influenza S. aureusMild: amoxicillin. Severe/admitted: ceftriaxone + azithromycin (atypical/Legionella); add vancomycin if MRSA, pip-tazo if Pseudomonas risk
HAPMore Gram-negatives, Pseudomonas, S. aureusAnti-pseudomonal coverage

CRB-65: Confusion, RR ≥30, low BP (SBP <90 / DBP ≤60), age ≥65. 0 → often outpatient; 3–4 → urgent admission. Hypoxemia matters even at low scores. Narrow on cultures (penicillin-sensitive pneumococcus → amoxicillin).

Tuberculosis

Think TB when
Chronic cough, fever, night sweats, weight loss, hemoptysis, or non-resolving pneumonia despite typical antibiotics, esp. from a high-incidence country.

Latent: TST or IGRA (don't use to diagnose active disease in adults). Active: CXR + sputum ×3 AFB smear/culture + susceptibilities + NAAT. Control: airborne isolation (negative pressure, N95), notify public health, contact tracing, directly observed therapy. Latent Rx: rifampin ×4 mo; active: RIPE then continuation.

Sepsis & meningitis

Sepsis = life-threatening organ dysfunction from dysregulated host response (SOFA ≥2). Septic shock = vasopressor need + high lactate despite fluids (mortality >40%). qSOFA (≥2 of: RR ≥22, altered mentation, SBP ≤100) screens for risk.

Sepsis bundle
Cultures fast → early broad-spectrum antibiotics → IV fluids to restore perfusion / clear lactate → vasopressors for shock → source control. Delays in appropriate therapy raise mortality.
Adult bacterial meningitis empiric: ceftriaxone + vancomycin (resistant pneumococcus) + ampicillin (Listeria in older/at-risk adults). Don't delay for the LP/culture.

guides.title: Weeks 12–13 · 7 GI, SSTI & Candidiasis
guides.html:
Gut, Skin & Yeast

GI, SSTI & Candidiasis

Weeks 12–13 · Micro & ID
CPC 1 · Year 1
Study Suite

Gastroenteritis and C. difficile (spores, contact + sporicidal, vancomycin/fidaxomicin), the SSTI ladder to necrotizing fasciitis, and invasive candidiasis (echinocandin first, pull the line, never a contaminant).

Gastroenteritis & C. difficile

Most non-C. diff infectious diarrhea is self-limited — supportive hydration, avoid unnecessary antibiotics. Fever/bloody diarrhea suggests invasive pathogens (Salmonella, Shigella, Campylobacter, EHEC).

C. difficile
After antibiotics/dysbiosis; watery diarrhea, abdominal pain, fever/leukocytosis; most common nosocomial enteric pathogen. Spores survive harsh environments → recurrence + need for sporicidal cleaning and contact precautions. Treat with oral vancomycin or fidaxomicin (metronidazole now less preferred); recurrent → fecal microbiota transplant.

Skin & soft tissue

SyndromeBugManagement
ImpetigoS. aureus, S. pyogenesTopical mupirocin / oral cephalexin
Erysipelas / cellulitisS. pyogenes, S. aureusCephalexin/cefazolin; find portal of entry (tinea pedis)
Furuncle / abscessS. aureus incl. MRSAIncision & drainage; antibiotics if cellulitis/systemic/immunocompromised
Bite woundsPolymicrobial + anaerobesAmoxicillin-clavulanate
Necrotizing fasciitisGAS, polymicrobial, clostridialSurgical emergency: debridement + broad antibiotics; penicillin + clindamycin for GAS; IVIG for TSS

Superficial swabs are low-yield in cellulitis; abscess aspirate/deep tissue/blood cultures help when severe. Invasive GAS → droplet/contact for 24 h after effective therapy.

Candidiasis

Candida is a normal commensal; superficial disease = thrush, esophagitis (HIV/malignancy), vulvovaginitis, intertrigo. Risk: diabetes, antibiotics, central lines, TPN, ICU, neutropenia.

Invasive candidiasis
Candida in blood is never a contaminant. Candidemia disseminates (eye, heart, kidney). Treat with an echinocandin empirically in ill patients, step down to fluconazole if susceptible/stable, remove/replace infected lines, and evaluate for disseminated foci (e.g. eye exam).

guides.title: Weeks 12–13 · 8 Viral Hepatitis, HIV & Exposures
guides.html:
Blood-Borne

Viral Hepatitis, HIV & Exposures

Weeks 12–13 · Micro & ID
CPC 1 · Year 1
Study Suite

Hepatitis B serology decoded (vaccine vs resolved vs acute vs chronic) and the anti-HCV→HCV-RNA rule, HIV acute seroconversion and test sequence, and needlestick management with PEP/PrEP.

Viral hepatitis

Nonspecific labs: ↑bilirubin, ↑ALT/AST; impaired synthesis = low albumin, prolonged INR. Serology is the cornerstone; molecular testing for viral load/genotype.

MarkerMeaning
HBsAgCurrent infection (>6 mo = chronic); infectious
Anti-HBsImmunity (vaccine or resolved); ≥10 IU/L protective
Total anti-HBcPast/current natural infection (not from vaccine)
IgM anti-HBcAcute infection / flare
Anti-HCVExposure only — need HCV RNA to prove active infection
HBV profiles
Anti-HBs only → vaccine immune. Anti-HBc + anti-HBs, IgM neg → resolved infection. HBsAg + IgM anti-HBc → acute. HBsAg >6 mo + anti-HBc, IgM neg → chronic.

Natural history: HAV no chronicity; HBV adult chronic ~5% / infant ~90%; HCV chronic 75–85% but mostly curable. HBV suppression: tenofovir/entecavir, monitor HBV DNA.

HIV

Retrovirus → CD4+ T-cell loss. Acute seroconversion: fever, lymphadenopathy, pharyngitis, rash, diarrhea after exposure. Diagnose with 4th-gen Ab/p24 Ag + viral load (HIV RNA detectable earliest, then p24, then antibodies). Treat ALL with ART (2 NRTIs + INSTI) — don't wait for CD4 <200.

Blood-borne exposure / PEP

IssueManagement
NeedlestickWash/flush, report immediately (don't wait); assess source/exposure
HIV PEPWithin 72 h (best <24 h), 4 weeks of 3 antiretrovirals; NOT for low-risk or a virologically suppressed source with no STI; test at 6 & 12 wk
Recurrent exposuresOffer PrEP, not repeated PEP
HBV exposureCheck anti-HBs; if nonimmune + source positive/unknown → vaccine ± HBIG
HCV exposureNo vaccine/Ig/prophylaxis; baseline + follow-up testing; early treatment

guides.title: Weeks 12–13 · 9 Fever, Travel, Parasites & Emerging Disease
guides.html:
Wider Lens

Fever, Travel, Parasites & Emerging Disease

Weeks 12–13 · Micro & ID
CPC 1 · Year 1
Study Suite

Fever physiology and rigors, the returned-traveller work-up (malaria first), virus/parasite classification, the emerging-disease drivers, and the structured re-think for a non-resolving infection.

Fever & FUO

Exogenous pyrogens → leukocyte IL-1/TNF/IFN/IL-6 → PGE2 → raised hypothalamic set point. Rigors occur because the body now perceives itself as too cold and shivers to generate heat. Differential: infection, malignancy, autoimmune, drug fever, thrombosis, endocrine, heat illness (blunted in elderly).

The returned traveller

Rule out malaria FIRST
Any febrile traveller from the tropics (esp. sub-Saharan Africa): thick/thin smears now, repeat q12h ×3, or malaria NAAT. Dengue (Central America/Caribbean, short incubation), enteric fever (South Asia), also consider acute HIV, hepatitis, rickettsia, schistosomiasis, TB.
IncubationThink
Short (<21 d)Dengue (3–5 d), Zika, bacterial enteritis, typhoid, acute HIV
Long (>21 d)Malaria, viral hepatitis, TB, schistosomiasis

Parasite & virus classification

  • Virus first split: DNA vs RNA (then envelope, capsid, replication). Enveloped viruses are less environmentally hardy.
  • Parasite hosts: definitive (sexual/adult) vs intermediate (larval/asexual) vs accidental.
  • Categories: protozoa, helminths (nematode/cestode/trematode), arthropods.

Emerging diseases & the non-resolving case

Emergence drivers: microbial adaptation, human susceptibility, land use/economic development, climate/weather, travel/commerce, public-health breakdown, intent to harm. Newly vs re-emerging vs deliberately emerging. Antigenic shift/reassortment drives pandemic influenza.

Non-resolving infection — reconsider
Bug-drug mismatch (resistant/atypical/TB/fungal/parasite), missing source control (abscess, line, necrosis, obstruction), wrong diagnosis (malignancy, PE, drug fever), host factors (immunocompromise — consider HIV + PJP), or a pharmacology problem (dose/route/absorption/adherence).

guides.title: Weeks 12–13 · 10 Cram Sheet
guides.html:
Night-Before High-Yield

Microbiology

Weeks 12–13 · Microbiology
CPC 1 · Year 1
Study Suite
★ Do-not-miss · first-step reflexes
Sepsis → resuscitate + cultures + broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1 h + source control
Bacterial meningitis → empiric ceftriaxone + vancomycin (+ dexamethasone; + ampicillin if Listeria risk) — don't delay for imaging/LP
Febrile neutropenia → empiric antipseudomonal antibiotics
Necrotizing fasciitis → surgical debridement + broad antibiotics
Severe C. difficile → oral vancomycin / fidaxomicin; stop the culprit antibiotic
HIV / blood-borne exposure → start post-exposure prophylaxis urgently

ID framework & stewardship Approach

Empiric → targeted broad first, then de-escalate on cultures
Cultures first draw before antibiotics where possible
Source control drain the abscess / remove the line
Stewardship narrowest effective agent, shortest course
Host immunocompromise / prosthetic material changes the differential

Micro basics & Gram stain Micro

Gram-positive cocci staph (clusters) · strep (chains)
Gram-negative rods enteric (E. coli, Klebsiella); Pseudomonas
Gram-negative diplococci Neisseria
Anaerobes abscesses, gut, mouth
Atypical / acid-fast no cell wall (Mycoplasma) · TB (acid-fast)

Infection control Public health

Transmission contact · droplet · airborne
Hand hygiene the single most effective measure
Isolation / PPE match to the route
Reportable notify public health; contact tracing

Antibacterials Antibiotics

Cell wall β-lactams (penicillins, cephalosporins, carbapenems); vancomycin
Protein synthesis macrolides, tetracyclines, aminoglycosides, clindamycin
DNA / folate fluoroquinolones; TMP-SMX
Anaerobes metronidazole (below diaphragm), clindamycin (above)
MRSA vancomycin, TMP-SMX, doxycycline, clindamycin
Pseudomonas pip-tazo, cefepime, carbapenem, cipro

Antifungal / antiviral / antiparasitic Other

Azoles fluconazole (Candida/Crypto); CYP interactions
Amphotericin severe systemic fungal; nephrotoxic
Echinocandins invasive candidiasis
Antiviral acyclovir (HSV/VZV); oseltamivir (influenza)
Antiparasitic agent depends on organism / travel

Respiratory / sepsis / meningitis Severe ID

CAP amoxicillin / macrolide; or respiratory fluoroquinolone
Severity CURB-65 guides admission
TB RIPE (rifampin, isoniazid, pyrazinamide, ethambutol); INH + B6
Meningitis ceftriaxone + vancomycin (+ dexamethasone)
Sepsis bundle cultures, lactate, fluids, early antibiotics

GI / SSTI / candidiasis GI & skin

C. difficile oral vancomycin or fidaxomicin; stop culprit antibiotic
Cellulitis strep/staph cover; add MRSA cover if risk
Diabetic foot polymicrobial; assess for osteomyelitis
Candidiasis fluconazole (azole)
Food poisoning usually supportive; toxin vs invasive

Viral hepatitis / HIV / exposures Bloodborne

HBV serology HBsAg (infection), anti-HBs (immune), anti-HBc (exposure)
HCV screen, confirm RNA; curable with DAAs
HIV combination ART (e.g. tenofovir + emtricitabine + 3rd agent)
Exposure PEP after exposure · PrEP for prevention
Vaccinate HBV (and at-risk groups)

Fever / travel / parasites Travel ID

Travel fever malaria, typhoid, dengue — take a travel history
Malaria thick / thin films; species-directed therapy
Eosinophilia think helminths
Incubation timing narrows the differential
Weeks 12–13 · Microbiology · cram sheet · cue → answer

facets.use: MRSA; empiric meningitis; severe C. difficile (oral)

facets.adverse: Metallic taste, disulfiram reaction with alcohol
Orange secretions, hepatotoxic, strong CYP inducer
Pulmonary toxicity (chronic); avoid if eGFR low or pyelonephritis
